title: Introducing workspace agents in ChatGPT
summary: Introducing workspace agents in ChatGPT | OpenAI Published: 2026-05-11T21:46:41+00:00 Summary Workspace agents, an evolution of GPTs, have been introduced in ChatGPT, enabling workspace agents to take on many tasks people already do at work, such as preparing reports, writing code, and respo
content: Introducing workspace agents in ChatGPT | OpenAI [...] 26- [...] Workspace agents, an evolution of GPTs, have been introduced in ChatGPT, enabling workspace agents to take on many tasks people already do at work, such as preparing reports, writing code, and responding to messages. They run in the cloud and can continue working even when you're away from work. They are designed to be shared within an organization and improve their work over time. Workspace agents can gather context from the right systems, follow team processes, ask for approval when needed, and keep work moving across tools. Agents are available in research preview in Chat GPT Business, Enterprise, Edu, and Teachers plans.

title: Perceptron Mk1 shocks with highly performant video analysis AI model 80-90% cheaper than Anthropic, OpenAI & Google
summary: Perceptron Mk1 shocks with highly performant video analysis AI model 80-cheaper than Anthropic, OpenAI & Google | VentureBeat Published: 2026-05-12T18:45:17.739000+00:00 Author: Carl Franzen Summary Perceptron Inc. has released its flagship proprietary video analysis reasoning model, Mk1
content: Perceptron Mk1 shocks with highly performant video analysis AI model 80-cheaper than Anthropic, OpenAI & Google | VentureBeat [...] Published: 2026-05-12T18:45:17.739000+00:00 [...] Perceptron Inc. has released its flagship proprietary video analysis reasoning model, Mk1 (short for "Mark One"), which is 80-cheaper than other leading proprietary rivals such as Anthropic, OpenAI & Google. The model is designed to process native video to 2 frames per second across a significant 32K token window, allowing it to extend its architecture to longer streams and maintain object identity even through occlusions. It is supported by industry-standard benchmarks focused on grounded understanding and is priced at $0.15 per million input tokens and $1.50 per million output tokens. The launch signals a new era where models are expected to understand cause-and-effect, object dynamics, and the laws of physics with the same fluency they once applied to grammar. [...] Perceptron' [...] . Early adopters [...] using the model [...] applications, such as [...] -clipping highlights from live sports, which leverages the model's temporal understanding to identify key plays without human intervention. In the robotics sector, [...] ating teleoperation episodes into training data, effectively automating the process of labeling and cleaning data for robotic arms and mobile units. Other use cases include multimodal quality control agents on manufacturing lines, which can detect defects and verify assembly steps in real-time, and wearable assistants on smart glasses that provide context-aware help to users. title: Google AI co-mathematician workbench for research
summary: Google AI co-mathematician workbench for research | ETIH EdTech News — EdTech Innovation Hub Google unveils AI co-mathematician as research agents move beyond chat 11 May The Google DeepMind and Google paper describes a Gemini-based system designed to help mathematicians manage open-ended
content: Google unveils AI co-mathematician as research agents move beyond chat [...] The Google DeepMind and Google paper describes a Gemini-based system designed to help mathematicians manage open-ended research, from literature search to failed hypotheses and proof review. [...] Google DeepMind and Google researchers have published a paper setting out AI co-mathematician, a Gemini-based agentic AI workbench designed to help mathematicians work through open-ended research problems. [...] The paper, titled AI Co-Mathematician: Accelerating Mathematicians with Agentic AI, describes a system that goes beyond a standard chatbot by giving researchers a stateful workspace where multiple AI agents can run parallel workstreams, track uncertainty, preserve failed attempts, search literature, test ideas, and produce mathematical working documents. [...] Google says the system helped early users solve [...] . It also reports a 48 [...] 4, which the paper [...] as a new high [...] systems evaluated. [...] The paper reports that AI co-mathematician scored 87 percent on an internal benchmark of 100 research-level mathematics problems with code-checkable answers. That compared with 57 percent for Gemini 3.1 Pro and 70 percent for Gemini 3.1 Deep Think. [...] On FrontierMath Tier 4, the system solved 23 out of 48 non-public sample problems, giving it a 48 percent score. Google says this was higher than the Gemini 3.1 Pro base model, which achieved 19 percent. [...] Google says AI co-mathematician is currently in a limited initial release, with a goal of developing future products that provide broader access. [...] Google unveils AI co-mathematician as research agents move beyond chat [...] Google Gemini now turns prompts into downloadable PDFs, Word docs, and spreadsheets

title: Anthropic unveils legal tools for Claude chatbot By Investing

content: Anthropic PBC has released a set of 12 new tools designed for legal professionals, including a commercial counsel tool for reviewing vendor agreements and a feature for bar exam preparation. The company also introduced integrations allowing Claude to connect with software from DocuSign Inc. and Thomson Reuters Corp. The features will be accessible to paying customers through Claude Cowork, an AI agent designed for office work, and third-party services built on Claude. The release of the tool in February led to a $1 trillion stock market decline. [...] 48. title: OpenAI Launches GPT-5.5 Instant as ChatGPT's New Default Model
summary: OpenAI Launches GPT-5.5 Instant as ChatGPT's New Default Model OpenAI Launches GPT-5.5 Instant as ChatGPT’s New Default Model 2026.05.09 SHARE OpenAI Launches GPT-5.5 Instant as ChatGPT’s New Default Model OpenAI has released a new foundation model called GPT-5.5 Instant, repla
content: OpenAI Launches GPT-5.5 Instant as ChatGPT's New Default Model [...] OpenAI Launches GPT-5.5 Instant as ChatGPT’s New Default Model [...] OpenAI Launches GPT-5.5 Instant as ChatGPT’s New Default Model [...] OpenAI has released a new foundation model called GPT-5.5 Instant, replacing GPT-5.3 Instant as the default ChatGPT model. [...] The update centers on reducing hallucinations (the generation of factually incorrect content) in sensitive areas such as law, medicine, and finance. [...] What’s Inside GPT-5.5 Instant [...] According to OpenAI, GPT-5.5 Instant maintains the low-latency performance of its predecessor while reducing hallucinations, with particular gains in law, medicine, and finance. [...] Benchmarks show meaningful jumps. On the AIME 2025 math competition test, the model scored 81.2 versus 65.4 for the previous model. On MMMU-Pro, a benchmark for image-and-text reasoning, it scored 76 versus 69.2. [...] The broader GPT-5.5 series was launched in April with improvements highlighted in coding and knowledge work. The new Instant version brings those gains to default-model users. [...] A new headline feature is improved context management. [...] GPT-5.5 Instant can use its search tool to reference past conversations, files, and Gmail, and return more personalized answers. The feature is rolling out first to Plus and Pro users on the web, with mobile to follow. OpenAI plans to expand access to Free, Go Business, and Enterprise users in the coming weeks. [...] For developers, the GPT-5.5 model is available through the API (application programming interface) as “chat-latest.” GPT-5.3 will remain as an option for paying users for three more months. [...] Key Takeaways [...] ・OpenAI released GPT-5.5 Instant as ChatGPT’s new default, with a focus on reducing hallucinations in law, medicine, and finance. [...] ・Benchmarks improved (AIME 2025: 65.4 → 81.2; MMMU-Pro: 69.2 → 76), and a new context feature references past conversations, files, and Gmail. [...] TechCrunch (GPT-5.5 Instant release) [...] TechCrunch (GPT-5.5 announcement in April)

title: Google Built an AI Mathematician — and It Immediately Solved a 60-Year-Old Open Problem
summary: Google Built an AI Mathematician — and It Immediately Solved a 60-Year-Old Open Problem Search Google DeepMind has unveiled the AI co-mathematician — an agent-based system built on Gemini 3.1 that doesn't just generate mathematical text but actively participates in the research process.
content: Google Built an AI Mathematician — and It Immediately Solved a 60-Year-Old Open Problem Search Google DeepMind has unveiled the AI co-mathematician — an agent-based system built on Gemini 3.1 that doesn't just generate mathematical text but actively participates in the research process. Topologist Marc Lackenby used it to close problem 21.10 from the Kourovka Notebook, an open compendium of group theory problems maintained since 1965 in Novosibirsk. Impressive — and actually true. Image by Grok for abit.ee Google DeepMind has built a system that works not as a clever chatbot but as a genuine research workspace for mathematicians. The AI co-mathematician runs on Gemini 3.1 and is organised hierarchically: a project coordinator at the top, workstream coordinators below it managing literature review, library development, and counterexample search, and at the bottom a set of specialised agents — a search agent, a coding agent, and Gemini Deep Think acting as a proof verifier. Lackenby fed the system problem 21.10 from the Kourovka Notebook: does every finite group have a "simply finite" presentation? The system attacked it from both directions simultaneously — trying to both prove and disprove it. The first proof document was flagged as flawed by one of the reviewer agents, which spotted a logical gap. Lackenby read the text, recognised a smart underlying strategy, took the hint from the critique, and pointed the system in the right direction. It then assembled a complete proof. A further review round found and corrected two minor errors. Concurrently, the system set a new record on the FrontierMath Tier 4 benchmark — correct answers on problems considered fiendishly difficult even for professional mathematicians. To be fair, the creators acknowledge limitations: reviewer agents sometimes converge on plausible but incorrect reasoning, and in the opposite case can fall into a "death spiral" of endless revision with no exit. Access remains restricted to a small group of testers. Other mathematicians who tested the system praised its utility, though the "elegance" of AI-generated proofs remains subjective. The core idea, as the authors put it: not an "oracle that delivers a solution in one shot," but a tool that holds research context over weeks and works alongside the mathematician. A step forward — modest, but real. More information: Google DeepMind
